reviewed.by: 2026-07-18 Tier-1 Evidence-Library explainer derived from certified library/kalam.html. apologia-orthodoxy CLEAN (0 heresy; Kalam rule intact — 'whatever begins to exist', explicit denial of 'everything has a cause'; science bounded 'corroborates not proves'; honest scope 'one load-bearing wall, not the whole house'; no deism; positive-case). Argument soundness inherited from the certified source essay.

voiceover: Where did everything come from? There are really only two options. Either the universe has always existed — or it had a beginning: a first moment before which there was, literally, nothing physical at all. The Kalam cosmological argument is just three lines. One: whatever begins to exist has a cause. Two: the universe began to exist. Therefore, the universe has a cause. Notice what it does not say — not 'everything has a cause,' only things that begin to exist. That precision is built in. Premise one: out of nothing, nothing comes. If things could pop into being from nothing, why don't we see it happening everywhere? Premise two is the real question: did the past stretch back forever, or begin? Two independent lines converge. First, philosophy: you can't cross an actual infinite by adding one moment at a time. Picture Hilbert's Hotel — infinitely full, yet always room for one more. The paradoxes signal that an actual infinite can't be built in the real world, one event after another. Second, science: the Big Bang traces the universe to a finite past. And in 2003, the Borde-Guth-Vilenkin theorem showed that essentially any expanding universe must have a past boundary. Stated honestly, the science strongly corroborates a beginning — it does not prove it. That is exactly why the two lines lean on each other. So the universe has a cause — one that brought all of space, time, and matter into being. It must therefore be spaceless, timeless, immaterial, unimaginably powerful, and itself uncaused. And most plausibly, a mind that freely chose to bring it into being. Kalam alone does not reach the God of the creed. It is one load-bearing wall — alongside fine-tuning, moral value, and the resurrection. But it puts the oldest question back on the table: the universe is not a self-explaining brute fact. And the heavens still declare something. The full case at apologiadaily.com.

lines.t: time, and matter — so it is
